name: rsi
whatItMeasures: The RSI measures the speed and magnitude of recent price changes to evaluate whether an asset is overbought or oversold. It oscillates between 0 and 100. Developed by J. Welles Wilder Jr. in 1978, it compares the average gain to the average loss over a lookback period, providing a normalized momentum reading.

parameters.name: Period
parameters.rationale: Wilder's original recommendation of 14 periods remains the standard. It balances responsiveness with smoothness — shorter periods create more noise, longer periods miss reversals.

howToRead: RSI below 30 indicates the asset is oversold — selling pressure may be exhausted and a bounce is possible.
RSI above 70 indicates the asset is overbought — buying pressure may be exhausted and a pullback is possible.
RSI around 50 is neutral and suggests no strong momentum in either direction.
Divergence between RSI and price (price makes new high but RSI does not) can signal an impending reversal.
In strong trends, RSI can remain overbought or oversold for extended periods — do not blindly counter-trade.

signalRules.buy: RSI crosses below 30 (oversold), especially when followed by a turn back above 30.
signalRules.sell: RSI crosses above 70 (overbought), especially when followed by a turn back below 70.
signalRules.neutral: RSI between 30 and 70 with no divergence or extreme reading.

limitations: In strong trending markets, RSI can remain overbought (>70) or oversold (<30) for long periods, generating premature exit signals.
RSI is a single-timeframe indicator and should be confirmed with trend indicators like SMA or MACD.
The 30/70 thresholds are guidelines, not precise reversal points — some practitioners use 20/80 for stronger signals.
RSI does not account for volume, which can provide important confirmation of price moves.
evidence: In the 100-year DJIA study by Liberatedstocktrader.com, RSI-based strategies consistently achieved high win rates. The RSI 14 with standard 30/70 thresholds showed reliable mean-reversion characteristics, particularly when combined with trend-following indicators as filters. Its strength lies in identifying exhaustion points in counter-trend moves.
